in case you're unfamiliar with singular learning theory:

reply_context.text: My pivot to "statistical learning *theory*" was to evoke *singular* learning theory. Classical stats (i.e. generalized linear modeling) is mainly non-singular. Modern theory extends this to singular models. Either way, we're talking about Fisher Information dynamics, which is ultimately statistics.

posts.text: My pivot to "statistical learning *theory*" was to evoke *singular* learning theory. Classical stats (i.e. generalized linear modeling) is mainly non-singular. Modern theory extends this to singular models. Either way, we're talking about Fisher Information dynamics, which is ultimately statistics.

reply_context.text: My impression is that for you, "statistics" largely means "descriptive statistics". For me, "statistics" means "characterizing uncertainty." As soon as we invoke an error estimate of any kind: we're invoking the statistical toolkit.

posts.text: My impression is that for you, "statistics" largely means "descriptive statistics". For me, "statistics" means "characterizing uncertainty." As soon as we invoke an error estimate of any kind: we're invoking the statistical toolkit.

reply_context.text: So even if we're looking at a classically ML method like the perceptron algorithm, if we want to characterize what's going on here the appropriate toolbox to perform that analysis is limiting behavior on random variables. Statistics is just how learning works.

posts.text: So even if we're looking at a classically ML method like the perceptron algorithm, if we want to characterize what's going on here the appropriate toolbox to perform that analysis is limiting behavior on random variables. Statistics is just how learning works.

reply_context.text: When I say all ML is better described as "statistical learning", what I'm asserting is that learning processes are fundamentally statistical procedures. That's just what learning is. The accumulation of evidence in service of discovering heuristics to inform a decision function of some kind.
